All right, you're back. Let's rock on. It's time now to want to our third protocol, which is I want you to explain to your customers exactly what type of people you're asking them to refer. Don't just assume that they know who to refer to you. Lay out the exact criteria of who it is that you're looking for so they can lead you to the exact right people. See, the more specific you are, the more they'll stop worrying that they're wasting their friend's time. This is really crucial and it'll also save you a ton of time. So don't feel bad about saying, you know, I'm looking for people who you either own their own home, anybody who has this might have problems with their insurance, whatever it might be. Just be specific. And again, what are you going to do? You're going to script this out and we'll get to that in more detail. I explained how you asked for them. But the point is you want to have all of this thought out before and stripped out exactly how you're going to say those words. Don't leave it up to chance. Okay, but the more specific you can get, the better off you are. And also the more time you'll save when you're not calling people who simply just are not right for your product. Okay, now next protocol. Okay, when you get a referral, so I want to Greece to give you one. I want you to actually ask them for an introduction rather than just contact information. See, if you can get your new client or your early client, right, to actually introduce you, it is so much better. Now best if it could be like either in person or on a phone call, or simply if maybe they have to send an email at worst or a text and CC, well, you know, you know, they put both you on one test, but it's so much better than you simply getting someone's name or their email address and it being a blind call. Okay, if they can get on the telephone or a text or a joint email, oh my God, it just gives you that much more credibility. And of course, your conversion rate will go up even higher. And also it allows you to maintain some degree of control over what your client says about you and your product. If you're kind of like right there, you can kind of control the outcome or else they might never try to hurt you, but they might explain things the wrong way. And then you might find yourself playing a little catch up or an uphill battle trying to explain why, you know, the customer didn't quite explain what you have to offer. So it allows you to make sure you get off to a perfect opening with this new potential customer. All right, now next, this is important. And of course, we're at scripting. I want you to remember the power of language. And for that matter, along with that is the idea of strategic preparation. Listen, I know I'm a broken record here, okay, but languages, everything it creates meaning. And of course, the tonality that you lay on top of that, these are the most powerful tools you have to control the outcome to control the conversation, so to speak, right? And there are certain words here that have proven to be very, very effective when it comes to getting referrals. Number one is, I want you to introduce me versus refer to me. It was don't say the only you could refer to me and say anyone you could introduce. So you refer to me has a sort of salesy edge to it. And there's money being exchanged. I'm not a negative connotation versus I'd like you to introduce me to a friend, your friend to friend introduction, right? Now, another word here, I want you to use the word help, as opposed to someone I can get or give. So I want to help someone out. You know, someone I can help out, someone I can help with my service nodes, if they have friends, you want to help their friends. Okay, takes away that business like, you know, monetary aspect. It's about giving referrals, but no, it's about simply joining one I could help anyone I could serve. Well, in the same way that I've helped us serve you, you get it? See, when I do that, it takes away the fact that, you know, I feel like, oh, like I'm asking a friend, and maybe I'm going to be getting a kickback, some negative connotation people have, right? When you take those out of the equation, you will find that people are very, very quick to refer their friends, their family, their neighbors, or their business associates. Now, like I said, a mouthful, why did I do that? Friends, family, neighbors, or business associates, guess what? I want you to use all four of those categories in one sentence. Why? I'll tell you why, because it almost tends to embarrass them into action. In other words, if someone's on the cusp of giving you a referral, right? And they're kind of thinking, searching their own database emotionally, right? Mentally, right? Well, what do you do? Well, instead of making them fill in the blanks, hey, do you know anyone who I could help in the same way I helped you, right? Make it multiple choice. Do you know any friends, family, any neighbors, business associates? In other words, you give them the choices, the props, and have them choose one. Multiple choice questions are a lot easier than fill in the blank. It's almost you're essentially jogging their memory, or force themselves to jog their own memory by throwing out a very wide net from you, making it very easy. And it's not like now, not pushy like, now listen, come on, you got some friends, family, neighbors, huh? Don't do it like that. It'll be very casual. You know, any of friends, any family, neighbors, business associates that could help in the same way that I've helped you? Casual. No big deal. Use all those words that any friends and family, neighbors, business associates, that I could help my program the same way that I've helped you. Powerful stuff. So again, the focus there is on giving them prompts, making it easy. Okay? And then lastly, ready? If possible, I want you to reward people for their support. Thank them. Now, here's the deal. You gotta be careful here, right? Now, if you're in a company that or an industry that does not allow payments of cash or any gifts, don't do it then. Do not break the law. Do not do anything unethical. But very often there are referral programs in place within your company. And there are certain legal protocols within your industry that allow you to incentivize people to direct business to you. I want you to max those out, but don't break any rules. Okay? But it can be the difference between success and failure in your business, especially when dealing with professionals like lawyers and accounts and financial planners, where they expect to get something in return, often rightfully so, but also there's going to be certain legal limits to what you can give and in the way that you can give them. In fact, very often it's about disclosure. You can give someone whatever you want to give them, but it's got to be disclosed. For instance, in the mortgage industry, you could pay someone for a referral, but it had to be actually noted in the closing course so the customer could see. Okay? Otherwise it could be, you know, considered an illegal kickback. So just be careful. Follow the rules on that, but I will tell you what, that by giving people monetary incentives to give you referrals, it can be a massive boon to your business. All right? Now, if you can't do that, what else can you give? Well, there are very inexpensive knick-knacks and, you know, chotchkes you call them, right? And even a simple thank you note to show that you care and that you appreciate what they've done. So again, even a sincere handwritten thank you note, you know, can go a long way, but I got to be true to money. A professional probably is going to want a bit more than that. All right? Again, you know, don't do anything that goes over the line. Just make sure you think this through. What gifts can you give? What type of monetary compensation can you provide legally? And that will be okay with your company. And also you might want to approach your company and say, Hey, are there any referral programs in place? And then make sure you use those to the max. Now, next, I want you to look at referrals, your next protocol, right? As an ongoing business. You ask someone right away. And then I want you to ask them again three months later by sending them out an email with a little gift or a little something, an ethical gift there. And you ask a person again, in other words, referrals are not a wham, bam, done. No. Every time the opportunity arises when you answer a question and resolve a problem, have an interaction is always a way you can weave in asking for referrals. Never gets old. And again, the mind boggling number is that 20, I think it was under 30, 29 was the number, right? Who knows if it's really exactly, right? But the point is under a third of people actually are asked to give referrals. And yet almost 91% of the people said they give them. What does that tell you? You know, that tells me massive untapped resource for your business. You want to be the salesperson that goes out there and does the better job, gives better customer service, closes in the right way using the straight line. So they're just so certain about you. And then when you ask for referrals in the right way, you get them in droves. You make this an ongoing business. You set that target to aim for, you know exactly what your numbers are, how many people you have to call each day, how many have to ask each day, right? And you will watch how you build this massive stream of customer referrals. And that will truly explode your business. All right. So what can I say?
